    Using H2O's AutoML Package in Python - A Demo¶

    H2O AutoML: Simplifying Machine Learning¶

    H2O-3 is a fast, open-source platform designed to make building machine learning models on large datasets both scalable and practical. Its in-memory architecture and distributed design make it well-suited for enterprise-grade predictive analytics.

    In response to the growing gap between the demand for ML expertise and the available talent, H2O has built tools that make machine learning more accessible—especially for non-specialists. Their AutoML platform goes beyond a basic interface and actually automates the process of training and tuning a wide variety of models, making it a practical solution for both newcomers and experienced data scientists.

    Streamlining the ML Pipeline¶

    H2O AutoML allows users to:

    • Automatically train and fine-tune many models within a defined time or model-count limit.

    • Skip manual hyperparameter tuning for tricky algorithms like deep neural networks.

    • Use a single, minimal interface—just point to your data and target column, then optionally set a time limit or model count.

    I find it valuable because it saves me development time by using AutoML’s wrapper function; thus freeing me up to focus on other tasks. For example, many of my causal inference use cases require a highly accurate prediction function as part of a broader modeling exerise but I usually don't have the bandwidth to build and evaluate a bunch of ML models. H2O takes care of this for me in a transparent way so I can focus my attention on getting the causal model right.

    Built-In Explainability¶

    Understanding models is made simple with built-in explainability tools. Whether you're looking at individual models or entire groups (like the best-performing ensemble), AutoML provides visual explanations with just one function call.

    Flexible Interfaces¶

    AutoML can be accessed through:

    • R and Python APIs using familiar parameters (x, y, training_frame, etc.)

    • H2O Wave’s Web UI for a code-free experience

    Requirements¶

    The H2O website contains a wealth of information about its architectue, data ingestion and manipulation, installation and user support documents, and videos. The following requirements are recommended for compatibility with H2O-3:

    Operating Systems:¶

    • Windows 7 or later
    • OS X 10.9 or later
    • Ubuntu 12.04
    • RHEL/CentOS 6 or later

    Languages:¶

    R and Python are not required to use H2O-3 unless you want to use H2O in those environments, but Java is always required (see below).

    • R version 3 or later
    • Python 3.6.x, 3.7.x, 3.8.x, 3.9.x, 3.10.x, 3.11.x

    Browser:¶

    An internet browser is required to use H2O-3’s web UI, Flow. Supported versions include the latest version of Chrome, Firefox, Safari, or Internet Explorer.

    numpy:¶

    H2O-3 only supports numpy<2.

    Java Requirements:¶

    H2O-3 runs on Java. The 64-bit JDK is required to build H2O-3 or run H2O-3 tests. Only the 64-bit JRE is required to run the H2O-3 binary using either the command line, R, or Python packages.

    Data¶

    The data used in this demo are from Kaggle and decribed as follows:

    This dataset has been designed as a benchmark for AutoML and predictive modeling in the financial domain. It focuses on assessing credit risk by predicting whether a borrower will experience serious delinquency within two years. The data comprises a mix of financial metrics and personal attributes, which allow users to build and evaluate models for credit risk scoring.

    It contains 10 features and a binary target indicating serious delinquency in the next 2 years (1=Yes, 0=No). The data has a Kaggle usability rating of 10, putting it amonth the best availalbe for download. The data set is not particularly large (11 columns, 16,714 rows) and leveraging VS Code's Data Wrangler extension, appears quite clean (no missing values, no notable outliers, etc.). The data is balanced with the target evenly split by 0s and 1s. Thus, the data are not representative of credit risk data found in the "wild" where event rates are well below 50 percent. Given the curator states "the data has been designed as a benchmark," I assume the data are synthetically generated. Nevertheless, based on my experience in credit card risk modeling, the selected feature distributions seem plausible. In short, good enough for a demo.

    AutoMl Demo¶

    After following the installation instructions for python, The following code imports H20, AutoML, and initializes the H20 cluster. The output provides details on the loading process.

    In [1]:
    Copied! 
    import h2o
from h2o.automl import H2OAutoML

h2o.init()
    import h2o from h2o.automl import H2OAutoML h2o.init()
    Checking whether there is an H2O instance running at http://localhost:54321..... not found.
Attempting to start a local H2O server...

    H2O has its own data loading and manipulaton tools. Since the target is binary, you have to inform H2O of that fact by using the asfactor() function. Otherwise, H2O will assume a numeric target is continuous and run rergressions instead of classifications. H2O can import many differet fule types and from many different platforms and the documentation for different use cases is excellent.

    In [ ]:
    Copied! 
    # Load the dataset
data = h2o.import_file("https://raw.githubusercontent.com/jbeells/infera-group/main/docs/projects/data/CreditRiskDataset.csv")

train, test = data.split_frame(ratios=[.8], seed=1234)

x = train.columns
y = "dlq_2yrs"
x.remove(y)

train[y] = train[y].asfactor()
test[y] = test[y].asfactor()
    # Load the dataset data = h2o.import_file("https://raw.githubusercontent.com/jbeells/infera-group/main/docs/projects/data/CreditRiskDataset.csv") train, test = data.split_frame(ratios=[.8], seed=1234) x = train.columns y = "dlq_2yrs" x.remove(y) train[y] = train[y].asfactor() test[y] = test[y].asfactor()
    Parse progress: |████████████████████████████████████████████████████████████████| (done) 100%

    Now the fun part! Training the models involves a couple of lines of code. The max_models=20 tells H2O to build 20 models in the AutoML run. There is another stopping parameter, max_runtime_secs, that specifies the maximimum run time. Both can be set and AutoMl will stop when either one is hit. The code here is minimal but AutoML has numerous optional parameters supporting a wide variety of use cases. It's worth noting that H2O a StackedEnsamnle algorithm by default. If ensamble overfitting is a concern, you can use the exclude_algos parameter and provide a list of exclusions (e.g., = ["StackedEnsamble", etc...]) Once the cell is run, depending on your resources, it would be a good time to step away for a coffee break!

    In [3]:
    Copied! 
    # Initialize and run AutoML
aml = H2OAutoML(max_models=20, seed=1234)
aml.train(x=x,y= y, training_frame=train)
    # Initialize and run AutoML aml = H2OAutoML(max_models=20, seed=1234) aml.train(x=x,y= y, training_frame=train)
    AutoML progress: |███████████████████████████████████████████████████████████████| (done) 100%

[tips]
Use `model.explain()` to inspect the model.
--
Use `h2o.display.toggle_user_tips()` to switch on/off this section.

    H2O provides a mountain of output diagnostics for the champion model (StackedEnsambnle, as expected). You will notice that the Gains/Lift Tables use a peculiar 16-quantile grouping scheme (at least it's odd to those of us use to the decile scheme). This is the default in H2O and supposedly reflects a balance between granularity and interpretabily. Sure.

    A much more concise and interesting summary output is the leaderboard. This provides a ranked listing of the models considered. It will usually be the case that the stacked ensemble models will be the top models. H2O AutoML produces two stacked models. One is based on all models trained (AllModels) and the top performing model from the family of algorithms considered (BestOfFamily). The third model would typically be the champion if StackedEnsembles were excluded. The cell below is run on the training sample and the following cell provides the leaderboard, diagnostics, and explainability metrics for the test sample.

    In [4]:
    Copied! 
    # View the leaderboard
lb = aml.leaderboard
lb.head(rows=lb.nrows)
    # View the leaderboard lb = aml.leaderboard lb.head(rows=lb.nrows)
    Out[4]:
    model_id auc logloss aucpr mean_per_class_error rmse mse
    StackedEnsemble_AllModels_1_AutoML_1_20250731_105517 0.856677 0.4724620.854434 0.2312990.3917510.153469
    StackedEnsemble_BestOfFamily_1_AutoML_1_20250731_1055170.856382 0.4732060.852978 0.22935 0.3920050.153668
    XGBoost_grid_1_AutoML_1_20250731_105517_model_2 0.856052 0.4733180.854042 0.2256820.3922320.153846

    Leaderboard

    Leaderboard shows models with their metrics. When provided with H2OAutoML object, the leaderboard shows 5-fold cross-validated metrics by default (depending on the H2OAutoML settings), otherwise it shows metrics computed on the frame. At most 20 models are shown by default.

    Confusion Matrix

    Confusion matrix shows a predicted class vs an actual class.

    StackedEnsemble_AllModels_1_AutoML_1_20250731_105517

    Confusion Matrix (Act/Pred) for max f1 @ threshold = 0.3433221686883272
    0 1 Error Rate
    0 1093.0 553.0 0.336 (553.0/1646.0)
    1 212.0 1412.0 0.1305 (212.0/1624.0)
    Total 1305.0 1965.0 0.2339 (765.0/3270.0)

    Learning Curve Plot

    Learning curve plot shows the loss function/metric dependent on number of iterations or trees for tree-based algorithms. This plot can be useful for determining whether the model overfits.

    Variable Importance

    The variable importance plot shows the relative importance of the most important variables in the model.

    Variable Importance Heatmap

    Variable importance heatmap shows variable importance across multiple models. Some models in H2O return variable importance for one-hot (binary indicator) encoded versions of categorical columns (e.g. Deep Learning, XGBoost). In order for the variable importance of categorical columns to be compared across all model types we compute a summarization of the the variable importance across all one-hot encoded features and return a single variable importance for the original categorical feature. By default, the models and variables are ordered by their similarity.

    Model Correlation

    This plot shows the correlation between the predictions of the models. For classification, frequency of identical predictions is used. By default, models are ordered by their similarity (as computed by hierarchical clustering). Interpretable models, such as GAM, GLM, and RuleFit are highlighted using red colored text.

    SHAP Summary

    SHAP summary plot shows the contribution of the features for each instance (row of data). The sum of the feature contributions and the bias term is equal to the raw prediction of the model, i.e., prediction before applying inverse link function.

    Partial Dependence Plots

    Partial dependence plot (PDP) gives a graphical depiction of the marginal effect of a variable on the response. The effect of a variable is measured in change in the mean response. PDP assumes independence between the feature for which is the PDP computed and the rest.
    Made with Material for MkDocs
